properties.remarks: The creator method returns the creator string which appears beneath an extension inside the Extensions Manager dialog.

properties.name: description
properties.summary: The description method returns the long description which appears beneath an extension inside the Extensions Manager dialog.
properties.remarks: The description method returns the long description which appears beneath an extension inside the Extensions Manager dialog.

types.name: String
types.summary: The String class contains a method used to parse a string as a length value. All string arguments in Ruby API expect utf-8 strings.

methods.name: to_l
methods.summary: The to_l method is used to convert a string to a length.
methods.remarks: The to_l method is used to convert a string to a length. The returned length is expressed in the Model units.

returns.doc: the length value

types.name: UI
types.summary: The UI module contains a number of methods for creating simple UI elements from a SketchUp Ruby script.

methods.name: add_context_menu_handler
methods.summary: The add_context_menu_handler method is used to register a block of code with SketchUp that will be called when a context menu is to be displayed.
methods.remarks: The add_context_menu_handler method is used to register a block of code with SketchUp that will be called when a context menu is to be displayed. The context menu handler can then display the context menu with the items that you have added. Be careful with what you do in a context menu handler. If you perform an operation takes take a long time, such as traversing the model or selection in a large model it will delay the menu. See the contextmenu.rb script in the Plugins/examples directory for an example.

returns.doc: the number of context handlers that are registered

methods.name: beep
methods.summary: The beep method plays a system beep sound.
methods.remarks: The beep method plays a system beep sound. The beep method does not accept any arguments nor return any values.

methods.name: create_cursor
methods.summary: Note: Since SketchUp 2016 it is possible to provide vector images for the cursors.
methods.remarks: Note: Since SketchUp 2016 it is possible to provide vector images for the cursors. SVG format for Windows and PDF format for macOS. This is the recommended format to use since it will scale well with different DPI scaling. Note: Because the image is never released, avoid creating new cursors for the same image for any given tool. Cache it and reuse it. The create_cursor method is used to create a cursor from an image file at the specified location. This must be called from within a custom tool. See the Sketchup::Tool documentation for a complete example. The size of the cursor images should be 32x32 pixels. Any size different from that will be scaled using the shrink-to-fit policy. The coordinates for the cursor's hotspot are based from it's top left corner, starting at (0, 0). For example, a value of (hot_x, hot_y) = (5, 10) would identify the hotpoint of the cursor at 5 pixels from the left edge of your cursor image and 10 pixels from the top edge of your cursor image.

params.name: path
params.doc: File path to an image.

params.name: hot_x
params.doc: The x-coordinate of the “hotpoint” of the cursor, computed from the left edge of your cursor image.

params.name: hot_y
params.doc: The y-coordinate of the “hotpoint” of the cursor, computed from the top edge of your cursor image.

returns.doc: Id associated with the cursor. Use this with set_cursor in Sketchup::Tool#onSetCursor.

throws.when: if @param hot_x or @param hot_y are negative or larger than the max value of an Integer.

methods.name: get_clipboard_data
methods.summary: Returns the plain text available on the clipboard, if there is any.
methods.remarks: Returns the plain text available on the clipboard, if there is any.

methods.name: inputbox
methods.signature: inputbox(prompts, defaults, title) => Array<String>, false | inputbox(prompts, defaults, list, title) => Array<String>, false
methods.summary: Note: The method intelligently handles various types for default values and lists, automatically attempting to convert provided inputs to match the type of the default value.
methods.remarks: Note: The method intelligently handles various types for default values and lists, automatically attempting to convert provided inputs to match the type of the default value. This ensures consistency in data types throughout the operation. Creates a dialog box for inputting user information. The dialog box contains input fields with static text prompts, optional default values, optional drop down selections, and optional title. You can also use this method to display drop down lists of options, by passing an optional param.

params.name: prompts
params.doc: An array of prompt names appearing in the input box adjacent to input fields.

params.name: defaults
params.doc: An array of default values for the input fields.

params.name: title
params.doc: The title for the input box.

returns.doc: An array of returned values if the user did not cancel the dialog. If the user canceled the dialog, false is returned. The returned values in the array will be in the same order as the input fields.

methods.name: inspector_names
methods.summary: The inspector_names method is used to returns the names of all the inspectors.
methods.remarks: The inspector_names method is used to returns the names of all the inspectors. Inspectors are another name for the various floating dialog windows that you can activate from withing SketchUp, such as the Materials window.

returns.doc: an array of strings containing the names of inspectors.

methods.name: menu
methods.summary: Note: The 'Extensions' menu was named 'Plugins' prior to SketchUp 2015.
methods.remarks: Note: The 'Extensions' menu was named 'Plugins' prior to SketchUp 2015. For backward compatibility 'Plugins' still works. Note: 'Developer' menu was added with SketchUp 2021.1. The menu method retrieves a SketchUp's menu object with a given name. This is the first step toward adding your own custom items to the bottom of SketchUp's menus. Valid menu names are: 'File', 'Edit', 'View', 'Camera', 'Draw', 'Tools', 'Window', 'Extensions', 'Help' and 'Developer'.

params.name: menu_name
params.doc: The name of a supported menu.

methods.name: messagebox
methods.summary: Creates a dialog box containing static text with a series of buttons for the user to choose from.
methods.remarks: Creates a dialog box containing static text with a series of buttons for the user to choose from. Valid message box types are: MB_OK - Contains an OK button. MB_OKCANCEL - Contains OK and Cancel buttons. MB_ABORTRETRYIGNORE - Contains Abort, Retry, and Ignore buttons. MB_YESNOCANCEL - Contains Yes, No, and Cancel buttons. MB_YESNO - Contains Yes and No buttons. MB_RETRYCANCEL - Contains Retry and Cancel buttons. MB_MULTILINE - Contains and OK button. Return values can be any of following: IDOK IDCANCEL IDABORT IDRETRY IDIGNORE IDYES IDNO In an MB_MULTILINE message box, the message is displayed as a multi-line message with scrollbars (as needed). MB_MULTILNE also allows a third string argument that will be used as the title for the messagebox.

params.name: message
params.doc: The message that you want to display.

params.name: type
params.doc: The message box type, which will be a constant from the list in the method comments.

returns.doc: A number corresponding to what the user selected.

methods.name: model_info_pages
methods.summary: The model_info_pages method is used to returns the names of all the available model info pages.
methods.remarks: The model_info_pages method is used to returns the names of all the available model info pages. These include UI windows such as Components, Credits, and Units.

returns.doc: an array of strings containing the names of model info pages.

methods.name: openpanel
methods.summary: The openpanel method is used to display the Open dialog box.
methods.remarks: The openpanel method is used to display the Open dialog box. The path that is returned can then be used inside code to open a text or image file. See the standard Ruby class File for examples of reading and writing from disk.

params.name: title
params.doc: The title to apply to the open dialog box.

params.name: directory
params.doc: The default directory for the open panel.

params.name: filename
params.doc: The default filename for the open panel. On Windows, you can alternatively pass a wildcard filter using this format: UIname|wildcard||. Additional filter dropdown list items can be added by adding additional pairs of filter name and filter like this: UIname1|wildcard1|UIname2|wildcard2||. Also multiple wildcard filters can be combined into a single line using a semicolon-separated list in the filter field: ui_name|wildcard1;wildcard2||.

returns.doc: the full path and name of the file selected, or nil if the dialog was canceled.

methods.name: openURL
methods.summary: The openURL method is used to open the default browser to a URL.
methods.remarks: The openURL method is used to open the default browser to a URL.

methods.name: play_sound
methods.summary: The play_sound method is used to play a sound file.
methods.remarks: The play_sound method is used to play a sound file. Valid sound files include .wav and .mp3 files on the Mac and .wav files on the PC.

params.name: filename
params.doc: the relative path to the filename from the SketchUp install directory, or an absolute path to the file. (See Sketchup.find_support_file for a way to search for a specific file.)

methods.name: preferences_pages
methods.summary: The preferences_pages method is used to returns the names of all the preferences pages.
methods.remarks: The preferences_pages method is used to returns the names of all the preferences pages. These include windows like Templates. SketchUp 2017 "Extensions" page was removed.

returns.doc: an array of strings containing the names of preference pages.

methods.name: refresh_inspectors
methods.summary: Tells SketchUp to refresh all inspectors such as the Component Browser and the Outliner.
methods.remarks: Tells SketchUp to refresh all inspectors such as the Component Browser and the Outliner. This is useful when you need to manually force a refresh after you've made a change to the document via Ruby. Generally, SketchUp will keep these in sync for you, but occasionally it does not, such as when model.start_operation has disabled UI updates.

methods.name: refresh_toolbars
methods.summary: Tells SketchUp to refresh all floating toolbars.
methods.remarks: Tells SketchUp to refresh all floating toolbars. This is useful when you need to manually force a refresh after you've made a change to the document via Ruby. Generally, SketchUp will keep these in sync for you, but occasionally it does not, such as when Sketchup::Model#start_operation has disabled UI updates. This only affects macOS, on Windows the toolbars are always refreshing.

methods.name: savepanel
methods.summary: The savepanel method is used to display the Save dialog box.
methods.remarks: The savepanel method is used to display the Save dialog box. The path that is returned can then be used inside code to save out a text or image file. See the standard Ruby class File for examples of reading and writing from disk.

params.name: title
params.doc: The title to apply to the save dialog box.

params.name: directory
params.doc: The default directory for the save panel.

params.name: filename
params.doc: The default filename for the save panel. On Windows, you can alternatively pass a mask, like “*.txt”, to have all the .txt files display. If you want multiple file types to display, you can supply multiple masks for the filename and separate them with a semicolon, like this: “.txt;.doc”.

returns.doc: the full path and name of the file selected or nil if the dialog was canceled.

methods.name: scale_factor
methods.summary: Note: SU2017M0 will automatically scale up line width and text size, but will not scale up the points provided to Sketchup::View#draw2d.
methods.remarks: Note: SU2017M0 will automatically scale up line width and text size, but will not scale up the points provided to Sketchup::View#draw2d. Returns the scaling factor SketchUp uses on high DPI monitors. Useful for things like Sketchup::View#draw2d.

methods.name: select_directory
methods.summary: The select_directory method is used to display the OS dialog for selecting one or several directories from the file system.
methods.remarks: The select_directory method is used to display the OS dialog for selecting one or several directories from the file system.

params.name: options
params.doc: The dialog can be customized by providing a hash or named arguments of options.

returns.doc: A string with the full path of the directory selected when :select_multiple option is set to false otherwise an array of strings or nil if the user cancelled.

methods.name: set_clipboard_data
methods.summary: Copies plain text to the clipboard.
methods.remarks: Copies plain text to the clipboard.

methods.name: set_cursor
methods.summary: The set_cursor method is used to change the cursor to a new cursor with a given cursor id.
methods.remarks: The set_cursor method is used to change the cursor to a new cursor with a given cursor id. See UI.create_cursor and the Tool class for details on creating your own tools with arbitrary cursors. If you call this while a standard SketchUp tool is active, you will not see your custom cursor, as these tools are constantly setting their own cursors to indicate SketchUp's state.

params.name: cursor_id
params.doc: The id of the cursor you want to display.

methods.name: set_toolbar_visible
methods.summary: The set_toolbar_visible method is used to set whether a given toolbar is visible.
methods.remarks: The set_toolbar_visible method is used to set whether a given toolbar is visible. Note that the toolbars and their names are different on the Mac vs. PC, so be careful and be sure to test when using this method in a cross-platform script.

params.name: name
params.doc: The name of a Ruby toolbar.

params.name: visible
params.doc: True to make the toolbar visible, false to hide it.

returns.doc: true if successful, false if not.

methods.name: show_extension_manager
methods.summary: The show_extension_manager method is used to display the Extension Manager dialog.
methods.remarks: The show_extension_manager method is used to display the Extension Manager dialog.

methods.name: show_extension_warehouse
methods.summary: Show the Extension Warehouse dialog inside SketchUp.
methods.remarks: Show the Extension Warehouse dialog inside SketchUp. If an extension UUID is provided the dialog navigates directly to that extension's page. If no UUID is provided it opens the Extension Warehouse home page.

params.name: extension_id
params.doc: Optional Extension Warehouse UUID. If nil or omitted the EW home page is shown.

methods.name: show_inspector
methods.summary: The show_inspector method is used to display the inspector with the given name.
methods.remarks: The show_inspector method is used to display the inspector with the given name. You can get the list of valid inspectors with UI.inspector_names.

params.name: name
params.doc: The name of inspector that you want to display.

returns.doc: true if successful, false if unsuccessful

methods.name: show_model_info
methods.summary: The show_model_info method is used to display the model info dialog for a specific page.
methods.remarks: The show_model_info method is used to display the model info dialog for a specific page. You can get the list of valid page names with model_info_pages. SketchUp 2014 "Classifications" page was added.

params.name: page_name
params.doc: The name of the model info dialog you want to display.

methods.name: show_preferences
methods.summary: The show_preferences method is used to display a SketchUp preferences dialog.
methods.remarks: The show_preferences method is used to display a SketchUp preferences dialog. You can get the list of valid dialogs with preferences_pages.

params.name: page_name
params.doc: The name of the preferences dialog you want to display.

returns.doc: true

methods.name: start_timer
methods.summary: The start_timer method is used to start a timer.
methods.remarks: The start_timer method is used to start a timer. This is an effective method to create a repeating snippet of code for arbitrary animation. See this blog post for an detailed example of custom animation using timers: sketchupapi.blogspot.com/2008/10/animate-yo-cheese.html Note that there is a bug that if you open a modal window in a non-repeating timer the timer will repeat until the window is closed.

params.name: seconds
params.doc: The time in seconds before your code should be called.

params.name: repeat
params.doc: true if you want the timer to repeat, false (or omit) if you do not want it to repeat.

returns.doc: a timer ID

methods.name: stop_timer
methods.summary: The stop_timer method is used to stop a timer based on its id.
methods.remarks: The stop_timer method is used to stop a timer based on its id.

params.name: id
params.doc: The timer id for the timer that you want to stop.

methods.name: toolbar
methods.summary: The toolbar method is used to get a Ruby toolbar by name.
methods.remarks: The toolbar method is used to get a Ruby toolbar by name. If the toolbar doesn't exist a new one will be created.

params.name: name
params.doc: The name of the Ruby toolbar.

returns.doc: a Toolbar object

methods.name: toolbar_names
methods.summary: The toolbar_names method is used to return the name of all the available native toolbars (this differs between PC and Mac).
methods.remarks: The toolbar_names method is used to return the name of all the available native toolbars (this differs between PC and Mac). These toolbar names do not include Ruby toolbars.

returns.doc: Array of strings representing toolbar names.

methods.name: toolbar_visible?
methods.summary: The toolbar_visible? method is used to determine whether a given toolbar is visible.
methods.remarks: The toolbar_visible? method is used to determine whether a given toolbar is visible. Note that the toolbars and their names are different on the Mac vs. PC, so be careful and be sure to test when using this method in a cross-platform script.

params.name: name
params.doc: The name of a native toolbar.

types.name: Command
types.summary: The Command class is the preferred class for adding tools to the menus and Ruby toolbars. For example, you could add a menu item and pass it a code block directly, or you could first create a Command. Using Commands gives you greater control over how the item works in the UI, and it allows multiple spots in the UI to call the same code. For example, You might want a toolbar button and a context-click menu item to both point to the same command, and to control the tooltip and its “graying” from a single spot in your code.

constructors.name: new
constructors.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2019 it was not possible to sub-class UI::Command due to a bug in how SketchUp initialized the class. The new method is used to create a new command.
constructors.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2019 it was not possible to sub-class UI::Command due to a bug in how SketchUp initialized the class. The new method is used to create a new command.

params.name: menutext
params.doc: The text that will appear for this command's menu item if it appears on a menu.

methods.name: get_validation_proc
methods.summary: The #get_validation_proc method returns the command's validation proc.
methods.remarks: The #get_validation_proc method returns the command's validation proc.

methods.name: proc
methods.summary: The #proc method returns the command's proc that is called when the command is invoked.
methods.remarks: The #proc method returns the command's proc that is called when the command is invoked.

methods.name: set_validation_proc
methods.summary: Note: Avoid disabling an command as it often isn't obvious to the user why it is disabled.
methods.remarks: Note: Avoid disabling an command as it often isn't obvious to the user why it is disabled. Prefer keeping the command enabled but show an error message if pressed when it cannot be used. The #set_validation_proc method allows you to change whether the command is enabled, checked, etc. For instance, the command toggling a dialog window may be displayed as checked while the dialog is open.

properties.name: extension
properties.summary: Note: This is an advanced feature that extension developers normally won't have to deal with.
properties.remarks: Note: This is an advanced feature that extension developers normally won't have to deal with. It's purpose is to address scenarios when SketchUp isn't able to automatically infer which extension the command belongs to. The #extension method returns the command's associated extension.

properties.name: large_icon
properties.summary: The large_icon method returns the icon file for the command's large icon.
properties.remarks: The large_icon method returns the icon file for the command's large icon.

properties.name: menu_text
properties.summary: The menu_text method returns the menu item name for the command.
properties.remarks: The menu_text method returns the menu item name for the command.

properties.name: small_icon
properties.summary: The small_icon method returns the icon file for the command's small icon.
properties.remarks: The small_icon method returns the icon file for the command's small icon.

properties.name: status_bar_text
properties.summary: The status_bar_text method returns the status bar text for the command.
properties.remarks: The status_bar_text method returns the status bar text for the command.

properties.name: tooltip
properties.summary: The tooltip method returns command item's tooltip text.
properties.remarks: The tooltip method returns command item's tooltip text.

types.name: Notification
types.summary: Notification objects allows you to show native notifications in the desktop. Notifications can have a message, icon and accept and/or dismiss buttons with callback blocks. Supported icon formats include: .bmp, .png, .jpg. Vector icons are supported as .svg on Windows and .pdf on Mac. Recommended icon size is 48x48 pixels. Icons larger than these sizes will be automatically downscaled to fit within the limits.
types.remarks: Known Bugs: Prior to SketchUp 2026.0 oversized icons are cropped on Windows.

constructors.name: initialize
constructors.signature: Notification(sketchup_extension, message = nil, icon_name = nil, icon_tooltip = nil)
constructors.summary: Note: In order to insert line breaks into the message you need to use \r\n. From SketchUp 2019 and onwards \n also works on both Mac and Windows. Creates a new UI::Notification object.
constructors.remarks: Note: In order to insert line breaks into the message you need to use \r\n. From SketchUp 2019 and onwards \n also works on both Mac and Windows. Creates a new UI::Notification object.

params.name: sketchup_extension
params.doc: SketchupExtension instance used to identify the source of the notification.

params.name: message
params.doc: Message to display.

params.name: icon_name
params.doc: Path to an icon to display along with the message.

params.name: icon_tooltip
params.doc: Tooltip for the icon.

methods.name: on_accept
methods.summary: Shows a button in the notification with the given title and callback block, both arguments are required.
methods.remarks: Shows a button in the notification with the given title and callback block, both arguments are required.

params.name: title
params.doc: Sets the title of the button.

params.name: block
params.doc: Sets the action callback, this will be called when the user clicks on the accept button.

throws.when: When calling on_accept when the notification has already been shown.

methods.name: on_accept_title
methods.summary: Returns the accept's button title.
methods.remarks: Returns the accept's button title.

methods.name: on_dismiss
methods.summary: Shows a button in the notification with the given title and callback block.
methods.remarks: Shows a button in the notification with the given title and callback block. Both arguments are required. This callback is only called if you press the Dismiss button, not when the time runs out and the notification automatically disappears.

params.name: title
params.doc: Sets the title of the button.

params.name: block
params.doc: Sets the action callback, this will be called when the user clicks on the dismiss button.

throws.when: When calling on_dismiss when the notification has already been shown.

methods.name: on_dismiss_title
methods.summary: Returns the dismiss's button title.
methods.remarks: Returns the dismiss's button title.

methods.name: show
methods.summary: Shows the notification.
methods.remarks: Shows the notification. If not interacted with, the notification will disappear without calling any callbacks.

properties.name: icon_name
properties.summary: Gets the icon name, this is the path that will be used to get the icon from the file system path.
properties.remarks: Gets the icon name, this is the path that will be used to get the icon from the file system path.

properties.name: icon_tooltip
properties.summary: Gets the icon Tooltip, this is the string that appear when the mouse is over the icon.
properties.remarks: Gets the icon Tooltip, this is the string that appear when the mouse is over the icon.

properties.name: message
properties.summary: Gets the message as string.
properties.remarks: Gets the message as string.

types.name: HtmlDialog
types.summary: Note: The window size is not guaranteed to be pixel perfect in all SketchUp versions and operating systems. Prefer responsive designs that can take up some fluctuations in size. The Ruby HtmlDialog class allows you to create and interact with HTML dialog boxes from Ruby. This is the best way to generate complex, embedded UIs inside SketchUp, but it does generally require HTML and JavaScript expertise. If your goal is to simple display a website to your users, consider using #openURL, which will show them a web page in their default browser rather than inside a dialog in SketchUp. The left, top, width, height etc. dimensions of the dialog are in logical units. This means you provide the units as if they where on a monitor with “normal” DPI. The units given will be multiplied by the same factor as returned by scale_factor. For usage examples, including how to migrate from the old WebDialog class, see github.com/SketchUp/htmldialog-examples. You may use the Trimble Modus framework for a look and feel of your dialog that matches that of SketchUp's dialogs. HtmlDialog uses the following versions of CEF (Chromium Embedded Framework): SketchUp 2025.0 CEF 128 SketchUp 2024.0 CEF 112 SketchUp 2021.1 CEF 88 SketchUp 2019.0 CEF 64 SketchUp 2018.0 CEF 56 SketchUp 2017.0 CEF 52 Starting with SketchUp 2026.1, HtmlDialog exposes a small set of built-in callbacks (mirroring the legacy skp: protocol used by WebDialog). sketchup.launchEW(id) – Opens the Extension Warehouse dialog and show a specified extension's page. sketchup.installRBZ(url) – Downloads and installs a Ruby extension from the given .rbz URL. (User will be prompted just like using the Extension Manager.)

constructors.name: initialize
constructors.summary: Note: Prefix preference_key with something unique to your extension. Note: If there is no reference kept to the HtmlDialog object, the window will close once the garbage collection runs. This behavior can be confusing in trivial test code but is usually not a concern in real life scenarios. Typically a persistent reference, e.g. an instance variable, should be kept to bring the dialog to front, rather than creating a duplicate, if the user should request it a second time. The new method is used to create a new HtmlDialog. In SketchUp 2021.1 use_content_size was added. When set to true, width, height, min_width, max width, min_height, max_height will represent the size of the content area of the window. This excludes the titlebar and the window frame. When use_content_size is set to false (the default value), the size dimensions will represent the outer frame size. The properties hash accepts an optional key style where the value is one of: UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_DIALOG HtmlDialog stays at the top of SketchUp. UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_WINDOW HtmlDialog can go behind SketchUp and doesn't disappear when SketchUp looses focus. UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_UTILITY HtmlDialog is shown with small titlebar and stays on top of SketchUp.
constructors.remarks: Note: Prefix preference_key with something unique to your extension. Note: If there is no reference kept to the HtmlDialog object, the window will close once the garbage collection runs. Typically a persistent reference, e.g. an instance variable, should be kept to bring the dialog to front, rather than creating a duplicate, if the user should request it a second time. The new method is used to create a new HtmlDialog. In SketchUp 2021.1 use_content_size was added. When set to true, width, height, min_width, max width, min_height, max_height will represent the size of the content area of the window. This excludes the titlebar and the window frame. When use_content_size is set to false (the default value), the size dimensions will represent the outer frame size. The properties hash accepts an optional key style where the value is one of: UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_DIALOG HtmlDialog stays at the top of SketchUp. UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_WINDOW HtmlDialog can go behind SketchUp and doesn't disappear when SketchUp looses focus. UI::HtmlDialog::STYLE_UTILITY HtmlDialog is shown with small titlebar and stays on top of SketchUp.

methods.name: add_action_callback
methods.summary: Note: When an HtmlDialog is closed, all callbacks to that instance are cleared.
methods.remarks: Note: When an HtmlDialog is closed, all callbacks to that instance are cleared. Attach or re-attach them before you show the dialog. The #add_action_callback method establishes a Ruby callback method that your html dialog can call to perform some function. Use the sketchup.callback_method_name to invoke the callback method from your html dialog. Your JavaScript in the html dialog will invoke the callback with the same number of arguments. The call is asynchronous. JavaScript call might return before Ruby callback even called. Use onCompleted callback to get notified for completion. Basic types such as booleans, numbers, strings, arrays and hashes are automatically converted between Ruby and JavaScript.

params.name: callback_name
params.doc: The name of the callback method to be invoked from the html dialog.

returns.doc: true if action added successfully, false otherwise.

Also see the Command object for details on creating “commands” which can be called from your toolbars.

types.name: WebDialog
types.summary: Deprecated. Please use HtmlDialog that was introduced in SketchUp 2017. The Ruby WebDialog class allows you to create and interact with DHTML dialog boxes from Ruby. If your goal is to simply display a website to your users, consider using openURL, which will show them a web page in their default browser rather than inside a dialog in SketchUp. See this blog post for a detailed, step-by-step example: sketchupapi.blogspot.com/2008/02/sharing-data-between-sketchup-ruby-and.html Under Windows the IE render mode is different in webdialogs than from what you see in the normal browser. It will by default pick an older render mode and different versions of SketchUp will use different modes. In order to reliably control the render mode of your webdialogs under Windows you need to insert a special META compatibility tag: Starting with SketchUp 2013, you can embed a special HTML link in your dialog that will launch Extension Warehouse and show a specified extension's page. This can be useful if your extension has a dependency on another one and you would like to direct the user to install that extension. For example, to launch an extension's page whose URL is: extensions.sketchup.com/en/content/advanced-camera-tools The link would be:

constructors.name: initialize
constructors.signature: WebDialog(dialog_title = "", scrollable = true, pref_key = nil, width = 250, height = 250, left = 0, top = 0, resizable = true)
constructors.summary: Note: Since SU2017 the position and size of the dialog is DPI aware - it will scale to the DPI of the monitor automatically. Specify units as they would be on a traditional low-DPI monitor. Note: The browser which is embedded inside the dialog depends on the user's OS. On Mac, Safari is embedded, while on the PC whatever version of Internet Explorer is installed will be embedded. The new method is used to create a new webdialog.
constructors.remarks: Note: Since SU2017 the position and size of the dialog is DPI aware - it will scale to the DPI of the monitor automatically. Specify units as they would be on a traditional low-DPI monitor. Note: The browser which is embedded inside the dialog depends on the user's OS. On Mac, Safari is embedded, while on the PC whatever version of Internet Explorer is installed will be embedded. The new method is used to create a new webdialog.

params.name: dialog_title
params.doc: The title to be displayed in the webdialog.

params.name: scrollable
params.doc: true if you want to allow scrollbars, false if you do not want to allow scrollbars.

params.name: pref_key
params.doc: The registry entry where the location and size of the dialog will be saved. If preferences_key is not included, the location and size will not be stored.

params.name: width
params.doc: The width of the webdialog.

params.name: height
params.doc: The height of the webdialog.

params.name: left
params.doc: The number of pixels from the left.

params.name: top
params.doc: The number of pixels from the top.

params.name: resizable
params.doc: true if you want the webdialog to be resizable, false if not.

methods.name: add_action_callback
methods.summary: The add_action_callback method establishes a Ruby callback method that your web dialog can call to perform some function.
methods.remarks: The add_action_callback method establishes a Ruby callback method that your web dialog can call to perform some function. Use the skp:callback_method_name to invoke the callback method from your webdialog. Your JavaScript in the webdialog will invoke the callback method with a string representing arguments to the callback method. Note that you're sending data down to Ruby as a single string that's passed via the window.location bar. In Internet Explorer on PC, there is a length limit of 2038 characters for this bar, so if you're needing to pass large data down you might consider using get_element_value to pull in a longer string from a hidden input field in the HTML.

params.name: callback_name
params.doc: The name of the callback method to be invoked from the webdialog.

returns.doc: nil

methods.name: allow_actions_from_host
methods.summary: By default, actions are only allowed on the host where the webdialog is displayed.
methods.remarks: By default, actions are only allowed on the host where the webdialog is displayed. The allow_actions_from_host method is used to selectively allow actions to take place on a host remote from the host where the webdialog exists. If the webdialog is local, no remote host is allowed unless you use this method.

params.name: hostname
params.doc: The name (domain) of the host that your webdialog can access safely.

methods.name: bring_to_front
methods.summary: The bring_to_front method is used to bring the webdialog to the front of all the windows on the desktop.
methods.remarks: The bring_to_front method is used to bring the webdialog to the front of all the windows on the desktop. See show_modal for how to ensure that your WedDialogs are on top.

methods.summary: The close method is used to close the webdialog.
